rationale_for_baseline_tier: Localised allergic contact dermatitis is benign and managed at home with allergen avoidance and a topical corticosteroid, with routine follow-up (T1). Secondary bacterial infection routes to GP/ED (T4); systemic hypersensitivity (not present) would be an emergency.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Redness spreading well beyond the contact area and becoming painful, thick yellow pus/honey-crusting, or fever; or whole-body hives / breathing difficulty
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same day — GP/ED (000 for airway/systemic features)
escalation_edges.rationale: Secondary bacterial infection needs antibiotics; systemic hypersensitivity needs emergency care.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the redness spreads well beyond the buckle area and becomes painful, oozes thick yellow pus, or you get a fever — see a doctor / go to ED that day. If you ever get whole-body hives or trouble breathing, call 000.

contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Provide clear secondary-infection red flags for self-monitoring.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Keep the metal off your skin (or barrier it), use the steroid cream, rinse gently with water and don't pop the blisters. It should settle over about a week.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Self-care + topical steroid; GP/ED for secondary infection
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: GP/ED for spreading painful redness, pus or fever; 000 for any airway/systemic features.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Allergic contact dermatitis (nickel, belt buckle); zoster and secondary infection screened negative; topical corticosteroid + allergen avoidance; patch testing if recurrent.

critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: A scratched, weeping contact dermatitis becomes secondarily infected and spreads unrecognised.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Not identifying/removing the allergen, guaranteeing recurrence.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: ED/ambulance for a localised contact dermatitis with a negative infection screen — unnecessary.
